schools.summary: The glorious woman: 'and there appeared a great wonder (sign) in heaven; a woman clothed with the sun, and the moon under her feet, and upon her head a crown of twelve stars' — Barnes (with Gill) holds the woman is the church/people of God, arrayed in the light and glory of Christ/the gospel ('clothed with the sun'), the changeable lower things or the old dispensation beneath her ('the moon under her feet'), crowned with twelve stars (the twelve apostles, or the patriarchs), the figure of the church glorious and exalted, treading the mutable world under foot, about to bring forth Christ; not the Virgin Mary but the covenant community. Gill: the church in the apostles' and purer ages, persecuted under the heathen and Arian powers.

Victorinus: the woman clothed with the sun, the moon under her feet, crowned with twelve stars, travailing in pain, is the ancient Church of fathers, prophets, saints, and apostles, which had the groans and longing until it saw Christ, the fruit of its people according to the flesh, take flesh out of that selfsame people; the sun the hope of resurrection and the glory of the promise, the moon the fall of the saints' bodies under death (which never wholly fails, having a light like the moon), the crown of twelve stars the choir of fathers from whom Christ was born. Bede: the church clothed with the light of Christ, treading temporal glory, bringing forth her children and travailing until Christ be formed in them (Galatians 4:19).

debates.question: Who is the woman clothed with the sun?

sides.position: The church / the covenant people of God who bring forth Christ (the Protestant and patristic consensus)
The Virgin Mary, who bore Christ (the Roman Catholic/Marian reading)

themes: a woman clothed with the sun, and the moon under her feet, and upon her head a crown of twelve stars
the woman clothed with the sun
the crux of the woman's identity
the church/covenant people, or the Virgin Mary

schools.summary: The figure of birth-pangs: 'and she being with child cried, travailing in birth, and pained to be delivered' — the woman in labor, crying out in the pains of childbirth (the figure of the church's longing, anguish, and effort to bring forth the Messiah, or more broadly her children, the travail of the people of God awaiting the promised seed); the travail signifying the suffering and earnest longing of the church/covenant people (in the patristic reading the groaning of the ancient church for the coming of Christ, Victorinus); 'pained to be delivered' — the woman on the point of bringing forth, the long-awaited birth of the man-child (v.5) at hand, the church's travail about to issue in the bringing forth of Christ, the anguish preceding the deliverance (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: she being with child cried, travailing in birth, and pained to be delivered
the woman in travail to bring forth
the church's anguish and longing to bring forth the Messiah
the travail preceding the deliverance

schools.summary: The adversary: 'and there appeared another wonder (sign) in heaven; and behold a great red dragon, having seven heads and ten horns, and seven crowns upon his heads' — over against the woman a great red dragon (identified in v.9 as Satan, the devil, the great adversary, 'red' marking his murderous, bloody character); the 'seven heads and ten horns, and seven crowns' marking the dragon's great power, dominion, and royalty (the same heads and horns appearing on the beast, chs. 13, 17, linking the dragon/Satan with the worldly persecuting power through which he works, in the historicist reading the heads and horns read of Rome, pagan and papal, as the dragon's instrument) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Victorinus: the dragon's red/purple color is the result of his work — 'from the beginning a murderer' (John 8:44), who has oppressed the whole human race not only by the bond of death but by various forms of destruction; the great enemy arrayed in power, ready to devour the woman's child, his heads and horns the marks of his usurped dominion over the world.

themes: a great red dragon, having seven heads and ten horns, and seven crowns upon his heads
the great red dragon with seven heads and ten horns
the murderous adversary arrayed in power
the devil working through the worldly persecuting power

schools.summary: The dragon's power and malice: 'and his tail drew the third part of the stars of heaven, and did cast them to the earth: and the dragon stood before the woman which was ready to be delivered, for to devour her child as soon as it was born' — the dragon's tail sweeping a third of the stars from heaven to earth (interpreters taking this of the great power of Satan drawing down a third, or of his casting down of angels in his fall, v.9, or of the casting down of teachers/leaders in the church, the figure of his vast destructive influence); the dragon poised before the woman to destroy the child the moment it is born (Satan's purpose to destroy the Messiah at his birth, as in Herod's slaughter of the infants and the attempts on Christ's life); the ancient enmity (Genesis 3:15) between the seed of the woman and the serpent coming to its crisis (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: his tail drew the third part of the stars of heaven, and did cast them to the earth
the dragon stood before the woman... for to devour her child as soon as it was born
the dragon casting down stars and waiting to devour the child
Satan's relentless purpose to destroy the Messiah

schools.summary: The birth and exaltation of the Messiah: 'and she brought forth a man child (son), who was to rule (poimanein — shepherd, rule) all nations with a rod of iron: and her child was caught up unto God, and to his throne' — the woman (the church/covenant people) bringing forth the man-child, identified as Christ by the distinctively Messianic title (Psalm 2:9, 'rule with a rod of iron,' Christ's universal, sovereign, irresistible rule over the nations); the male child unmistakably the Messiah, born of the people of God; and 'caught up unto God, and to his throne' — Christ, having been born and escaped the dragon's malice, exalted (his ascension to God's throne), the figure compressing Christ's birth, escape, and ascension/exaltation, the man-child enthroned with God beyond the dragon's reach (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Cyril: the man-child caught up to God and his throne is Christ, born of the people of God, who escaped the dragon's devouring malice and was exalted to the Father's throne; the child destined to rule the nations is the Messiah, snatched up to glory beyond the reach of the adversary who sought to destroy him.

themes: she brought forth a man child, who was to rule all nations with a rod of iron
her child was caught up unto God, and to his throne
the man-child born to rule and caught up to God
Christ born, delivered from the dragon, and exalted

schools.summary: The church's flight and preservation: 'and the woman fled into the wilderness, where she hath a place prepared of God, that they should feed her there a thousand two hundred and threescore days' — after the child is born and exalted, the woman (the church) fleeing to the wilderness, to a place God has prepared (the wilderness signifying a place of refuge, obscurity, and divine protection during persecution, God providing for his church in the time of trial); the church fed/nourished/sustained in the wilderness for 1260 days (the same period as the 42 months, 11:2, the witnesses' testimony, 11:3, and the 'time, times, and half a time,' v.14, read by historicists as 1260 years of the church's wilderness-state under the apostate power); God sustaining his church through the long appointed period of oppression, whether literal 3.5 years or 1260 years (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: the woman fled into the wilderness, where she hath a place prepared of God
that they should feed her there a thousand two hundred and threescore days
the woman fleeing to the wilderness for 1260 days
God preserving his church through the appointed period of trial

schools.summary: The war in heaven: 'and there was war in heaven: Michael and his angels fought against the dragon; and the dragon fought and his angels' — a war in heaven described, Michael leading his angels against the dragon and his angels (the cosmic conflict between the forces of God and of Satan); 'Michael' (the archangel, Daniel 10:13, 21; 12:1; Jude 9) the leader of God's angelic host, the prince and protector of God's people (many taking him as the great archangel; some, with Henry leaning, taking Michael as a name or title of Christ himself, the captain of the heavenly host, fighting for his church); 'the dragon fought and his angels' — Satan and his fallen angels warring against Michael and his host, the conflict between the two angelic armies, the figure of the great spiritual warfare in which the dragon is opposed and overthrown (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: there was war in heaven: Michael and his angels fought against the dragon; and the dragon fought and his angels
the war in heaven between Michael and the dragon
Michael the archangel (or Christ) leading God's host
the great spiritual warfare against Satan

schools.summary: The dragon's defeat: 'and prevailed not; neither was their place found any more in heaven' — the dragon and his angels defeated, not prevailing against Michael's host, their place no longer found in heaven (cast out, losing their standing); 'prevailed not' — Satan overcome in the conflict (despite his power, unable to stand against Michael and the heavenly host, the defeat of the adversary); 'neither was their place found any more in heaven' — the dragon and his angels losing their place in heaven, cast out (v.9), Satan who had access as the accuser (Job 1; Zechariah 3) now expelled, the figure of his decisive defeat and loss of standing, leading to his being cast down to earth (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: and prevailed not; neither was their place found any more in heaven
the dragon's defeat and expulsion from heaven
Satan overcome, unable to stand against Michael's host
the decisive defeat and loss of standing

schools.summary: The identification and expulsion: 'and the great dragon was cast out, that old serpent, called the Devil, and Satan, which deceiveth the whole world: he was cast out into the earth, and his angels were cast out with him' — the dragon plainly identified as the 'old serpent' (of Eden, Genesis 3), the 'Devil' (diabolos — the slanderer/accuser), and 'Satan' (the adversary), the various names marking his character as the ancient enemy, accuser, and adversary of God and man; 'which deceiveth the whole world (the whole inhabited earth)' — Satan's characteristic work being deception, leading the whole world astray (the deceiver of mankind); 'cast out into the earth, and his angels with him' — Satan and his angels cast down from heaven to the earth, his defeat in the heavenly war issuing in his being hurled to earth where (vv.12-13) he vents his rage, the figure of his decisive overthrow yet his continued limited activity on earth (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: the great dragon was cast out, that old serpent, called the Devil, and Satan, which deceiveth the whole world
he was cast out into the earth, and his angels were cast out with him
the dragon identified as Satan and cast down to earth
the ancient deceiver overthrown and hurled down

schools.summary: Heaven's triumph-song: 'and I heard a loud voice saying in heaven, Now is come salvation, and strength, and the kingdom of our God, and the power of his Christ: for the accuser of our brethren is cast down, which accused them before our God day and night' — at Satan's overthrow a loud voice proclaiming that salvation, power, and the kingdom of God and the authority of his Christ have come (the victory over the accuser the triumph of God's kingdom); 'the accuser (kategor) of our brethren is cast down, which accused them before our God day and night' — Satan's role as the accuser (Job 1:6-11; Zechariah 3:1) of the saints, perpetually and ceaselessly charging them before God, now ended (he cast down, no longer able to accuse, the deliverance of the brethren from their relentless accuser cause for the loud rejoicing of heaven) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Now is come salvation, and strength, and the kingdom of our God, and the power of his Christ
the accuser of our brethren is cast down, which accused them before our God day and night
heaven's triumph-song at the casting down of the accuser
the brethren freed from their relentless accuser

schools.summary: The ground and means of victory: 'and they overcame him by the blood of the Lamb, and by the word of their testimony; and they loved not their lives unto the death' — the brethren overcoming Satan, the accuser, by the blood of Christ (the atoning blood of the Lamb the ground of their victory, answering every accusation, cleansing from sin, and silencing the accuser, the believer's triumph resting on Christ's blood); 'by the word of their testimony' — their faithful confession of Christ and the gospel, their steadfast witness even unto death, the means by which, in the strength of the Lamb's blood, they overcome the adversary; 'they loved not their lives unto the death' — the martyr-spirit, the overcomers not clinging to their lives but willing to die for Christ, their willingness to lay down life for the testimony sealing their victory (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Cyril: the saints conquered the adversary not loving their lives even unto death — the martyr's self-sacrifice the seal of their victory; by the blood of the Lamb and their faithful testimony, and by their willingness to die rather than deny Christ, they overcame the accuser, esteeming the testimony of Christ above their own lives.

themes: they overcame him by the blood of the Lamb, and by the word of their testimony
they loved not their lives unto the death
the saints overcoming Satan by the blood of the Lamb and their testimony
victory grounded in Christ's blood and sealed by faithful self-sacrifice

schools.summary: The joy and the woe: 'therefore rejoice, ye heavens, and ye that dwell in them. Woe to the inhabiters of the earth and of the sea! for the devil is come down unto you, having great wrath, because he knoweth that he hath but a short time' — heaven and its inhabitants called to rejoice at Satan's casting down (the victory over the accuser cause for the gladness of the heavenly host); while heaven rejoices, woe falling on the earth, for the devil, cast down, coming with great wrath (his expulsion from heaven turning his fury upon the earth, the earth becoming the scene of his raging); his wrath intensified by his knowledge that his time is short (aware of his coming final defeat, he vents his fury the more fiercely in the brief time remaining, his rage real but limited and doomed) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: rejoice, ye heavens, and ye that dwell in them. Woe to the inhabiters of the earth and of the sea
the devil is come down unto you, having great wrath, because he knoweth that he hath but a short time
heaven's rejoicing and earth's woe at the devil's coming down
Satan's fury intensified yet limited by his short time

schools.summary: The turn of Satan's fury: 'and when the dragon saw that he was cast unto the earth, he persecuted the woman which brought forth the man child' — finding himself cast down to earth, the dragon turning his fury against the woman (the church) who bore the man-child (Christ); unable to destroy the child (now exalted, v.5), Satan persecuting the church (the church becoming the target of his wrath, his revenge for failing against Christ, the persecution of the church the dragon's vengeance); 'which brought forth the man child' — the church attacked precisely because she is the mother of the Messiah, the people of God from whom Christ came, the dragon's hatred of Christ turned against the community that bore and belongs to him (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: when the dragon saw that he was cast unto the earth, he persecuted the woman which brought forth the man child
the dragon persecuting the woman after his casting down
Satan venting his fury on the church
the church attacked as the mother of the Messiah

schools.summary: The deliverance and preservation: 'and to the woman were given two wings of a great eagle, that she might fly into the wilderness, into her place, where she is nourished for a time, and times, and half a time, from the face of the serpent' — the church given eagle's wings (the figure of swift, God-given deliverance, drawn from Exodus 19:4, 'I bare you on eagles' wings'; Deuteronomy 32:11) to flee from the dragon to her prepared place in the wilderness (God enabling the church to escape the persecutor); 'nourished for a time, and times, and half a time' (Daniel 7:25; 12:7) = 3.5 'times,' the same period as the 1260 days (v.6) and the 42 months (11:2), read by historicists as 1260 years (the church sustained by God through the whole period of her wilderness-flight); 'from the face of the serpent' — preserved away from the presence and reach of the dragon, God's care keeping the church from Satan's fury during the appointed time (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Cyril: the woman, borne on eagle's wings to her place in the wilderness, is nourished and kept safe from the face of the serpent for the appointed time — God preserving his church from the dragon's reach during the season of persecution, the wilderness her God-given refuge where Satan cannot devour her.

themes: to the woman were given two wings of a great eagle, that she might fly into the wilderness
where she is nourished for a time, and times, and half a time, from the face of the serpent
the woman given eagle's wings to flee and be nourished
God preserving his church from the serpent through the appointed period

schools.summary: The serpent's flood: 'and the serpent cast out of his mouth water as a flood after the woman, that he might cause her to be carried away of the flood' — the serpent pouring out a flood of water from his mouth after the fleeing woman, to sweep her away (the flood the figure of an overwhelming assault — persecution, false teaching, armies, or the rage of nations — by which Satan seeks to destroy the church); the 'flood' out of the serpent's mouth a torrent of opposition (interpreters taking it of floods of persecution, or of errors and heresies, or of hostile armies/peoples, waters often symbolizing peoples, 17:15); the serpent's aim to overwhelm and destroy the church by the torrent ('carried away' — swept off, destroyed), Satan's renewed attempt to annihilate the fleeing woman, the assault real and dangerous but (v.16) thwarted (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: the serpent cast out of his mouth water as a flood after the woman
that he might cause her to be carried away of the flood
the serpent's flood loosed against the woman
the torrent of persecution or hostile peoples to overwhelm the church

schools.summary: The earth's rescue: 'and the earth helped the woman, and the earth opened her mouth, and swallowed up the flood which the dragon cast out of his mouth' — the earth coming to the woman's aid, opening to swallow the flood (the torrent loosed to destroy the church absorbed and rendered harmless, God providentially delivering the church from the overwhelming assault); 'the earth helped the woman' — some earthly agency or providential circumstance coming to the church's rescue (in the historicist reading perhaps the absorption of the persecuting flood by the breakup of empires, the swallowing up of the invaders, or the diversion of the hostile powers, God using even the earth to protect his church); 'swallowed up the flood' — the torrent drained away, the assault failing, whatever Satan loosed to drown the church absorbed and neutralized, the dragon's renewed attempt thwarted, the church again preserved (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: the earth helped the woman, and the earth opened her mouth, and swallowed up the flood
the earth swallowing the flood and helping the woman
God providentially delivering the church from the assault
the dragon's renewed attempt thwarted

schools.summary: The dragon's war on the remnant: 'and the dragon was wroth with the woman, and went to make war with the remnant of her seed, which keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ' — frustrated in destroying the woman herself, the dragon (enraged) turning to war against the rest of her offspring (unable to destroy the church as a whole, Satan attacking her individual members, the faithful believers); 'the remnant of her seed' the woman's other children, the body of faithful believers, the individual Christians who are her offspring (the dragon, foiled against the church corporately, making war on the saints personally, the ongoing persecution of the faithful); the seed marked by 'keeping the commandments of God' and 'having the testimony of Jesus Christ' — the two marks of the true church and faithful believer (obedience to God's commandments and the holding/witnessing of the testimony of Jesus), the objects of the dragon's war and the description of those who, despite his fury, remain faithful (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: the dragon was wroth with the woman, and went to make war with the remnant of her seed
which keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ
the dragon making war on the remnant of the woman's seed
Satan's war against the obedient, witnessing saints
